stem: Think about the last time a useful idea showed up — in conversation, reading, on a walk. What happened to it?
options: It came and went; I didn't catch it
I remembered roughly what it was but not the specifics
I captured the gist somewhere but it's probably lost its edge
I captured it cleanly, in a form I can still return to and understand

stem: Where raw ideas actually go when you have them.
options: There's no real place — they stay in my head or get lost
They land in several scattered places I rarely consolidate
They mostly go to one place, though sometimes I default to another
One dependable place I trust to find them in again

stem: When something worth capturing slips past you, the usual reason is:
options: I didn't notice it was worth capturing until later
I noticed but told myself I'd remember — and didn't
I noticed but capturing in the moment felt like too much friction
This doesn't really happen; capture is a reflex now

stem: Looking across what you've captured recently — notes, voice memos, fragments:
options: I can't really look; it's too scattered or I haven't opened it
I can see it but it's a jumble; nothing stands out as connected
I can spot a few themes when I look carefully
I can name the threads that keep appearing and point to where

stem: How often you actually revisit what you've captured.
options: Almost never — things go in and stay there
Only when I'm looking for something specific
Occasionally, without a real rhythm
On a regular rhythm I return to — weekly or similar

stem: When you spot the same idea appearing in several of your notes:
options: I leave it alone — it feels like extra work
I notice it but don't do anything with it
I sometimes pull the threads together when the mood strikes
I treat it as a signal worth working on and refine it into something new

stem: When you go looking for something you captured three months ago:
options: I usually can't find it — or don't bother trying
I can sometimes find it if I remember roughly where I put it
I can find it, though it takes some digging
I can find it in under a minute because my system supports it

stem: The way your captured material is grouped or tagged.
options: It isn't — everything sits in one undifferentiated pile
Some of it is grouped loosely but inconsistently
Most of it is grouped in a way that mostly works
Grouped deliberately, in a way that makes retrieval and connection easier

stem: Your relationship to your capture system right now.
options: It feels chaotic and I avoid looking at it
It's functional but I'm aware it's drifting
It works well enough that I keep using it
I actively maintain it because I know its value compounds

stem: When you look at a month of captured ideas and have to choose which deserves real work:
options: I can't tell which matter most; they all feel equivalent
I pick based on what excites me most in that moment
I can usually identify two or three with more weight than the others
I can name why each promising one is promising, and why the others aren't

stem: The gap between what you've captured and what you've actually pursued.
options: Vast — collection is easier than pursuit, and I feel the weight of it
Large — I have a backlog of ideas I've never touched again
Moderate — I pursue some, let most rest
Deliberate — I let ideas rest on purpose, and return to some with clearer intent

stem: The way you move from many captured ideas to the one that gets worked on.
options: No process — I pick whatever's loudest on the day
I pick but often regret the pick later
I have a rough way of working through candidates
I have a method I trust, and I use it regularly

stem: Think of a recent idea of yours. Could you describe what it actually is to someone outside your head?
options: Not really — it's still a feeling or a flash
Roughly, but I'd struggle to make it specific
Yes, with some effort and a few false starts
Yes, in a way they'd recognise and could question

stem: Turning a rough thought into something specific.
options: It tends to stay rough; I'm not sure how to sharpen it
I can sharpen it but it loses something in the process
I can sharpen it while keeping what made it interesting
Sharpening is part of how I develop ideas; I do it as I go

stem: Finding the real problem underneath an idea.
options: I'm usually working with the surface version of the problem
I can see there's something deeper but can't quite get to it
I can dig under the surface with effort and time
I instinctively look for the problem underneath, not the brief on top

stem: Think about the last idea you got excited about. How soon did you try to find what was wrong with it?
options: I didn't — challenging it felt like spoiling it
Much later, after I'd already sunk time into it
At some point, once the excitement settled
Early — finding the weaknesses is how I know whether it's real

stem: The methods you have for testing whether an idea is actually useful.
options: None really — I just keep thinking about it
I ask a few people I know, informally
I've got a couple of go-to ways to get external input
I have a toolkit I use — examples, tests, people, evidence paths

stem: The way you compare your idea against alternatives that already exist.
options: I don't really compare — I focus on my own version
I do a quick search if I remember
I have a habit of scanning the landscape before committing
I use a repeatable method — tools, lists, examples — to map what's out there

stem: If someone asks who your idea is for, can you answer without hedging?
options: Not really — I tend to say it's for a lot of people
I can name a broad group but not a specific person
I can describe a specific kind of person clearly
I can name the person, their situation, and why they'd care

stem: Explaining the value of the idea in plain language.
options: Mine comes out tangled or abstract
It's okay but uses language that loses most people
I can say it plainly but it still feels incomplete
I can say it plainly and it lands — people recognise the problem

stem: An idea that sounds interesting but doesn't have a clear purpose.
options: I tend to build it anyway because it's interesting
I build it but start doubting halfway through
I notice the purpose problem and try to locate it before building
I hold it as unfinished thinking and don't confuse it with a buildable idea

stem: The last time you had to pick between two ideas worth doing:
options: I kept deferring the decision and nothing moved
I picked the one that felt easier or louder in the moment
I picked based on rough reasoning I could articulate afterwards
I picked using a clear set of criteria I trust

stem: Stopping work on an idea that isn't strong enough.
options: I struggle to stop — I keep trying to rescue it
I stop eventually but it costs me more time than it should
I can stop when I see it clearly, even if reluctantly
I treat stopping as part of the work; the sunk time isn't wasted

stem: Knowing when an idea is ready to move into building.
options: I'm never sure; I tend to keep thinking or just launch anyway
I move when I feel ready, which is sometimes too early or too late
I have rough markers I look for before I commit to building
I have clear criteria for readiness and trust them

stem: When a developed idea needs to become a first practical step:
options: I stall — I can think about it but can't find where to start
I find a start, but often the wrong one, and loop back
I can usually find a reasonable first step
I find the smallest useful first step and commit to it quickly

stem: Breaking an idea into buildable parts.
options: I can't see the parts — it still feels like one big thing
I can break it down but the parts feel arbitrary
I can break it into a rough sequence that works
I break it into parts where each one is buildable and testable on its own

stem: Starting to build before every detail is clear.
options: I wait for clarity that rarely arrives
I start but get stuck at the first ambiguity
I can start with partial clarity and figure it out as I go
Starting with partial clarity is how I get clarity

stem: The tools you have at your disposal for making:
options: Limited — I work with what I have, and it often doesn't quite fit
Enough for basics but I'm often reaching for something I don't have
Mostly good — I have what I need for the work I do
Well-stocked and deliberate — my tools actively support what I make

stem: Using AI tools to make real progress on what you're building.
options: I've barely used them or don't know where to start
I use them for small bits but don't trust them for the real work
I use them confidently for parts of the build
I direct them — they work to my instructions and I can tell when they're off

stem: When a new tool promises to help but you're already mid-build.
options: I drop what I'm doing to try it
I get distracted often enough that builds stall
I can bookmark it and stay with the build most of the time
I stay with the build; new tools wait until there's a reason to switch

stem: Think about what you've made in the last few months. What's the ratio of finished to unfinished?
options: Mostly unfinished; I have more starts than endings
About half and half; finishing is a struggle
I finish more than I don't, though often later than I meant to
I finish regularly; finishing is part of the practice, not the exception

stem: Deciding when something is ready to share.
options: I never feel it's ready; I keep polishing or hide it
I decide based on whether I'm tired of it, which isn't the same thing
I decide when it does what it needs to do, even if it could be better
I have a clear definition of done for each thing and ship at that point

stem: Releasing work into the world.
options: It stays in my drafts; release feels exposing
I release sometimes but with significant reluctance
I release when things are ready, without dragging it out
I release consistently; each release teaches me something for the next

stem: Think about the last thing you released. What did you learn from it?
options: I haven't really thought about it — I moved on
A vague sense of what worked or didn't, but nothing specific
Two or three specific things I'd do differently
Clear learning I've already applied to what I'm making now

stem: The systems you have for getting feedback on what you've made.
options: I don't seek it out; feedback arrives by chance or not at all
I ask a few friends or people I trust, informally
I have one or two reliable places I can test something
I use a range of feedback channels deliberately for different kinds of work

stem: Keeping momentum after the first release.
options: I rarely come back; the energy dissipates
I come back if something pulls me — feedback, external need
I plan the return before releasing, so momentum carries
Release is mid-cycle, not the end; momentum is built in
